input.background: Exercise 11.11 calculated the mean length of menstrual cycles in an SRS of $n=9$ women. The data revealed $\bar{x}=27.78$ days with standard deviation $s=2.906$ days.
input.data: Exercise 11.11 ：Menstrual cycle lengths (days) in an SRS of nine women are as follows: $\{31,28,26,24,29,33,25,26,28\}$. Use this data to test whether mean menstrual cycle length differs significantly from a lunar month. (A lunar month is 29.5 days.) Assume population values vary according to a Normal distribution. Use a twosided alternative. Show all hypothesis-testing steps.
input.question: Based on the confidence interval you just calculated, is the mean menstrual cycle length significantly different from 28.5 days at $\alpha=0.05$ (two sided)? Is it significantly different from $\mu=30$ days at the same $\alpha$-level? Explain your reasoning.

output.answer: To test significance, check if 28.5 or 30 falls within the calculated confidence interval. If either value falls outside the interval, it is significantly different at $\alpha=0.05$ level.

sample_data.description: Menstrual cycle lengths in days for a sample of nine women.

sample_mean.description: Mean length of menstrual cycles in the sample.

sample_standard_deviation.description: Standard deviation of menstrual cycle lengths in the sample.

sample_size.description: Number of women in the sample.

lunar_month_length.description: Length of a lunar month in days.

hypothesized_mean_1.description: First hypothesized mean menstrual cycle length to test against.

hypothesized_mean_2.description: Second hypothesized mean menstrual cycle length to test against.
